01a_quotes.title: Shifting Work Patterns with Generative AI

study_design: We present evidence from a field experiment across 66 firms and 7,137 knowledge workers.

Between September 2023 and October 2024, Microsoft partnered with 66 large firms to run a cross-industry field experiment to measure how access to an integrated generative AI tool, Microsoft 365 Copilot (hereafter, Copilot), changed work patterns.
Each firm recruited at least 100 workers for random assignment to treatment and control groups.
Treated workers received access to this new generative AI tool while controls continued working with their current technologies.
Firms were asked to maintain the random assignment for six months, giving treated workers time to explore the new tool and integrate it into their daily work.

Our main estimation is a difference-in-differences (DiD) approach, which uses pre-experiment data to account for baseline individual heterogeneity and increases precision relative to a simple comparison of means.
Our baseline estimates omit months 0-3 (weeks 0-12) after each firm's experimental licenses were assigned to focus on the longer-term effects of Copilot use.
We also estimate local average treatment effects by instrumenting a Copilot use indicator with treatment assignment.
Because we test multiple hypotheses, we use sharpened q-values that account for the increased false discovery rate as the number of tests increases (Anderson, 2008).
task: Workers were randomly selected to access a generative AI tool integrated into applications they already used at work for email, meetings, and writing.
Treated workers were free to use or not use the tool during their regular work.

Participants in the study came from varied occupations, but all did work that used the Microsoft Office tools into which Copilot was integrated (e.g., emailing, video meetings, chat, document creation).
We use telemetry data collected by Microsoft products to track workers' behavior during the experiment and for several months beforehand.

Our primary outcome measures are related to time use (e.g. time spent in meetings or reading emails) and quantities of activities (e.g., emails read, meetings attended, documents authored) within the Microsoft Office suite, as we do not observe the content of any work nor any measures of productivity or performance evaluation.
population: We present evidence from a field experiment across 66 firms and 7,137 knowledge workers.
Appendix A includes more details on the 7,137 participating workers (3,684 treated) across 66 firms.
The firms span a range of industries (see Figure A.1a). All are very large and most operate in multiple countries.
Firms opted into the study in response to only modest incentives and may be more proactive in adopting new technologies than the average firm.
Appendix Table B.1 tabulates occupations for the subset of workers for whom we have that information.
As expected, the sample is dominated by knowledge workers who regularly do the kinds of emailing and word processing tasks for which Copilot was designed.
Our sample of workers seems roughly comparable to theirs. For example, their studied workers attended a mean of 39 meetings per month while ours attended 43.
Nonetheless, we caveat that participating workers may not be representative of the overall worker population at participating firms, or of knowledge workers more broadly.
The vast majority of workers in our sample used Outlook regularly (6,485 out of 7,137) in the pre-period months.
Our baseline sample for email outcomes includes 6,441 workers for whom we observed email sessions in the experiment period and any pre-period Outlook use.
Our main Word sample includes 2,525 workers who meet these criteria.
Our main Teams sample includes 6,170 workers in the remaining firms.
arms: Treated workers received access to this new generative AI tool while controls continued working with their current technologies.
Each firm recruited at least 100 workers for random assignment to treatment and control groups.

Within this group, Microsoft's marketing team recruited firms to join the study with the understanding that they would allocate at least 50 of these 300 licenses to the experiment and recruit at least twice as many participating workers.
The Copilot licenses were assigned at random among experiment-eligible workers, but treated workers were not required to use the tools or deploy them for specific work tasks.
outcome_definitions: Our primary outcome measures are related to time use (e.g. time spent in meetings or reading emails) and quantities of activities (e.g., emails read, meetings attended, documents authored) within the Microsoft Office suite, as we do not observe the content of any work nor any measures of productivity or performance evaluation.
Outlook total session time The time spent aggregated across all Outlook sessions in a week, measured in hours.
Outlook sessions The number of Outlook sessions in a week.
Email-free work time The total time spent during an individual's working day in non-Outlook blocks at least 30 minutes long, measured in hours.
Outlook out-of-hours session time The subset of Outlook session time that occurred outside the individual's working day - measured in hours.
Unique email threads replied to The number of unique email threads/conversations among emails that are received that week and replied to within 7 days.
Time to reply from email delivery The average length of time it takes to reply to an email among emails received that week and replied to within 7 days, measured in hours.
Emails read The number of distinct emails read within a week.
Teams meeting time The time spent in scheduled Teams meetings (excluding spontaneous calls) in a week.
Total Teams meetings attended The number of Teams meetings attended in a week.
Share of meetings attended early/late (%) The share of scheduled Teams meetings joined either ≥5 minutes after the Teams meeting was started or left ≥5 minutes before the meeting was ended.
Word total session time The time spent in Word sessions, identified as blocks of actions with < 15 minute gaps.
Avg. document time to complete The average time between the creation (first edit) and completion of a document, for documents completed that week and where the target worker is the primary editor.
Completed documents The number of documents completed in a week where the target worker is the primary editor.
Total out-of-hours session time The sum of Outlook, Teams, and Word out-of-hours session time, representing a maximum of the out-of-hours activity on these apps.
statistical_results: Over 90% of the workers assigned to receive Copilot used it at least once in the 6 month period after treatment.
Usage peaked within the first 5 weeks of treatment assignment, possibly because of training, novelty, or trial motives.

To provide evidence on the effects of AI use when workers were already familiar with the tool, our main analysis focuses on outcomes at least 12 weeks after treatment assignment, where weekly average use stabilized at just under 40% of treated workers.
The median treated worker used Copilot in 39% of the weeks they had access to it during the study (33% of the weeks in months 4-6).
5% of treated workers used Copilot every week, while 20% never used it at all.
Overall usage peaked at 55% of treated workers in the first weeks of the study as workers explored the new tool and, at some firms, participated in introductory training sessions.
In telecommunications and technology firms, 45% of treated workers used Copilot each week, but only 25% did at construction and manufacturing firms (see Figure 1b).

Usage rates varied widely and consistently across firms, with average usage rates among treated workers as low as 6.3% per week at one firm over the final 3 months of the study and as high as 70% at another.
Individual pre-experiment activity in Outlook, Teams, and Word explains only 9.2% of the variation in Copilot adoption for all treated workers in months 4-6.
The share of coworkers that have Copilot, capturing firms' strategy in deploying these early licenses, adds little explanatory power.

Firm-level averages of the same pre-period work behavior explain 8.7% of the variation in adoption alone, with explanatory power loading on similar behaviors, and 12% with the individual-level averages.
Firm fixed effects alone explain nearly twice as much variation in individual workers' Copilot use as workers' pre-period work behavior.
Combining the two sets of predictors explains 21% of the variation in adoption rates.
Industry fixed effects explain only 2.9% of the variation, far less than firm fixed effects.
Industry fixed effects and indicators for the month each firm entered the study explains 8% of the variation.
Together, these factors explain 15% of the variation in Copilot use, still less than firm fixed effects alone.
In the pre-period, the average worker spent 11.7 hours per week reading and responding to emails.

Our intent-to-treat (LATE) estimates show workers with access to Copilot spent an average of 1.4 (2) fewer hours per week in email sessions over post-period months 4-6, a 12% (17%) decline from their pre-period average.

Workers with access to Copilot also consolidated their email work, reducing their count of Outlook sessions per week by 11% (17%), while opening up almost 1.5 hours per week of time in "non-email" blocks of 30 minutes or more during the regular workday with no Outlook activity (LATE = 2.2 hours).
The ITT results rule out effect sizes outside of the interval between -0.01 and 0.21 hours for Teams meetings relative to a mean of 5.22 hours per week.

We estimate an increase in Word session time of 0.14 (LATE = 0.19) hours per week, an 8.6% increase from a small pre-period mean, but this effect just misses statistical significance using the sharpened q-values.
Workers spent a quarter of an hour less per week working outside of their modal work hours, a 9% reduction, with somewhat larger time savings in after-hours email work.
Workers with Copilot read 4% (LATE = 6%) fewer emails each week (Table 2) with no increase in their time spent per email.
We can rule out increases larger than 1.2% in time per email in the ITT estimates.

No significant changes in the number of unique threads replied to or in the time from delivery to reply, suggesting that workers were not ignoring their inboxes or missing emails that required a reply.
We can rule out effect sizes greater than 3.4% of the mean in the ITT estimates for email threads and greater than 4.1% of the mean for reductions in time to reply to emails.
The number of emails per thread did not change, suggesting that Copilot may have been used for email summaries but with limited effects on the mix of emails or on thread efficiency.
We find no evidence for these kinds of changes for Teams tasks in Table 2 (we can rule out increases in the number of meetings greater than 4.6%).

Regular Word users with Copilot completed their documents more quickly, consistent with Noy and Zhang (2023), but the ITT point estimate is a savings of only 2% of the pre-period mean, and we cannot rule out that document time to complete increased by as much as 7%.

We estimate that Copilot use also slightly increased the total Word documents authored by the studied workers (we can rule out increases larger than 11% of the pre-period mean and cannot rule out no change).
Time to complete fell the most for collaborative documents (over 2 days, or a 17% reduction), however even these effects are not statistically significant using the sharpened q-values.
Any time savings are not accompanied by an increase in collaborative document production or the share of authored documents that include collaborators.
30% of studied workers have at least one treated coworker during the post-period.

The point estimates are very similar to the reduced form estimates in Table 1, have the same patterns of statistical significance based on the sharpened q-values, and generally fall within the confidence intervals of the original estimates.
The spillover effects on coworkers' email activity generally have the same sign as the main effects.
Adjusting for multiple hypothesis testing, the only statistically significant estimate is on unique email threads replied to, which falls.
Overall, these spillover estimates suggest that treated workers were using Copilot to reduce email time in efficient ways, with modest benefits for their coworkers.
In keeping with the largely null direct effects of Copilot for meetings or writing tasks, there are no spillover effects of Copilot access on connected coworkers' Teams or Word activity.

The point estimates suggest some modest benefits to gaining access to the new tool along with colleagues, treated workers with treated colleagues save 50% more time on email, but again these effects are imprecisely estimated and, with the sharpened q values, we cannot rule out zero differences in effect.

Treated workers did reduce their time spent in digital applications outside of their regular working hours by about 0.25 hours-per-week (9%), suggesting that some of the time saved on email returned to workers.
We do not find any negative spillover effects on the work patterns of treated workers' close coworkers, suggesting that the time savings we document do not come at the expense of work quality.
ambiguity_or_caveats: Although treated and control workers may have used other generative AI tools (for example, ChatGPT had been available for nearly a year by the beginning of the study), Copilot integrated AI into applications the workers already used regularly in their jobs.
We suspect that our sample excludes the highest-priority Copilot users, who would have received licenses with certainty rather than by lottery.
However, our estimates may still overstate population average treatment effects if firms targeted the experiment to workers where they expected to see large gains.

Unfortunately, our data use agreements do not allow us to compare these sampled workers with the broader set of workers at the firms, but we can compare our sample with the knowledge workers studied in Impink, Prat and Sadun (2025).
Nonetheless, we caveat that participating workers may not be representative of the overall worker population at participating firms, or of knowledge workers more broadly.
One limitation of this study is that we only observe activity within the Microsoft Office products we can track.
We are, for example, measuring time spent in meetings through Teams, not time spent in meetings overall.
Fortunately, the firms invited to participate in the study are all heavy users of Microsoft products.
In contrast, many sample workers show almost no Word activity during our pre-period months.

Based on conversations with participating firms and the patterns of application use by occupation in Table B.1, we think this mostly reflects roles that do not involve document creation, though some low activity could reflect use of a different word processing tool.
We do not expect (and do not observe) that individual access to Copilot would shift many of these workers towards using Word.
If it did, that change from zero to positive activity would have a very different interpretation than the intensive margin behavioral changes of workers with recorded Word activity in the pre-period.

We therefore restrict all Word-related analyses to workers with moderate use in the pre-period, indicated by above-median time spent in Word (0.25 hours/week) and above-median number of documents originated as the 'primary editor' (0.16 documents/week).
In the case of meetings, workers with near-zero pre-period use of Teams are concentrated in 10 firms, all of whom report primarily using non-Microsoft applications for video meetings.
As with document creation, we are unable to establish reliable pre-period baseline meeting activity for these workers.
In this case, we believe this largely reflects our inability to measure work meetings rather than true zeros.
Any Copilot-induced changes in Teams activity is likely to reflect substitution to Teams rather than true changes in work meetings.
We therefore drop workers at these 10 firms for all meeting outcome analyses.
Importantly, we do not have complete measures of workers' actions composing and sending email, so time spent creating emails must be inferred as the time between interactions with the reading pane.
Workers may multi-task or switch rapidly between applications such that sessions in different applications overlap.
Due to data processing challenges, we do not measure or adjust for this overlap across apps, so session-based measures may overstate the actual time spent on each activity.
Without observing the content of the emails they write, we cannot say definitively whether this time savings came at the cost of reduced work quality.
However, we can provide suggestive evidence by considering spillover effects on the colleagues of treated workers.
Note that Copilot use is strongly correlated across applications (see Appendix Table C.1), limiting our power to detect cross-application relationships.
We believe differences in Word use reflect the range of jobs done by our sample of workers.
Our smaller sample of regular Word users leaves us somewhat under-powered to detect changes in document-related work.

We cannot reject the null of 0 treatment effect heterogeneity for total Outlook session time when we allow the model to choose which dimensions of potential heterogeneity matter via elastic net or a neural networks approach.

Group average treatment effects for the top and bottom quintiles of workers by CATE are only modestly different than the full average treatment effects in Table 2 (-1.65 and -1.18 hours for the most and least affected workers) and we cannot reject that treatment effects for these two groups are equal.
Workers may also have close colleagues who received one of the firms' discretionary (i.e. not randomly assigned through the experiment) Copilot licenses.
Under our data use agreements, we are unable to track this Copilot access for workers outside the study.
Even our "high saturation" teams had, on average, only 39% of coworkers with access to Copilot, so we may simply be unable to detect team-level effects from this experimental design.
While not reported, we also find little change in activity in PowerPoint or Excel.
Apart from these individual time savings, we do not detect shifts in the quantity or composition of workers' tasks resulting from individual-level AI provision.

01a_quotes.notes: This is a large-scale field experiment conducted across 66 firms with 7,137 knowledge workers. The study measures the effects of access to Microsoft 365 Copilot on work patterns using telemetry data from Microsoft Office applications. The main finding is that workers who used Copilot spent approximately 2 fewer hours per week on email (a 17% reduction) and reduced out-of-hours work, but showed no significant changes in the quantity or composition of other tasks (Teams meetings, Word documents). The paper uses difference-in-differences estimation with intent-to-treat (ITT) and local average treatment effect (LATE) specifications, and applies sharpened q-values to account for multiple hypothesis testing. The experiment ran from September 2023 to October 2024, with the main analysis focusing on months 4-6 after treatment assignment when usage had stabilized.

01_extraction.title: Shifting Work Patterns with Generative AI
01_extraction.study_design_description: A field experiment across 66 firms and 7,137 knowledge workers conducted between September 2023 and October 2024. Each firm recruited at least 100 workers for random assignment to treatment and control groups. Treated workers received access to Microsoft 365 Copilot integrated into applications they already used (email, meetings, writing), while controls continued with their current technologies. Firms maintained random assignment for six months. The main estimation uses a difference-in-differences approach with pre-experiment data to account for baseline heterogeneity, focusing on outcomes at least 12 weeks after treatment assignment. Sharpened q-values were used to account for multiple hypothesis testing.
01_extraction.primary_task_description: Workers were randomly selected to access a generative AI tool (Microsoft 365 Copilot) integrated into applications they already used at work for email, meetings, and writing. Treated workers were free to use or not use the tool during their regular work. Participants came from varied occupations but all did work using Microsoft Office tools into which Copilot was integrated (emailing, video meetings, chat, document creation). Telemetry data from Microsoft products tracked workers' behavior during the experiment and for several months beforehand.
01_extraction.population_description: 7,137 knowledge workers across 66 large firms spanning multiple industries and countries. The sample is dominated by knowledge workers who regularly do emailing and word processing tasks for which Copilot was designed. Participating workers may not be representative of the overall worker population at participating firms or of knowledge workers more broadly. The vast majority of workers (6,485 out of 7,137) used Outlook regularly in the pre-period. Baseline samples for specific outcomes: 6,441 workers for email outcomes, 2,525 for Word outcomes, and 6,170 for Teams outcomes.

arms.description: Treated workers received access to Microsoft 365 Copilot integrated into applications they already used at work for email, meetings, and writing. Copilot licenses were assigned at random among experiment-eligible workers, but treated workers were not required to use the tools or deploy them for specific work tasks.
Control workers continued working with their current technologies without access to Copilot.

outcomes.outcome_description: The time spent aggregated across all Outlook sessions in a week, measured in hours.
The number of Outlook sessions in a week.
The total time spent during an individual's working day in non-Outlook blocks at least 30 minutes long, measured in hours.
The subset of Outlook session time that occurred outside the individual's working day, measured in hours.
The number of unique email threads/conversations among emails that are received that week and replied to within 7 days.
The average length of time it takes to reply to an email among emails received that week and replied to within 7 days, measured in hours.
The number of distinct emails read within a week.
The time spent in scheduled Teams meetings (excluding spontaneous calls) in a week.
The number of Teams meetings attended in a week.
The share of scheduled Teams meetings joined either ≥5 minutes after the Teams meeting was started or left ≥5 minutes before the meeting was ended.
The time spent in Word sessions, identified as blocks of actions with < 15 minute gaps.
The average time between the creation (first edit) and completion of a document, for documents completed that week and where the target worker is the primary editor.
The number of documents completed in a week where the target worker is the primary editor.
The sum of Outlook, Teams, and Word out-of-hours session time, representing a maximum of the out-of-hours activity on these apps.

Our intent-to-treat (LATE) estimates show workers with access to Copilot spent an average of 1.4 (2) fewer hours per week in email sessions over post-period months 4-6, a 12% (17%) decline from their pre-period average.

Workers with access to Copilot also consolidated their email work, reducing their count of Outlook sessions per week by 11% (17%), while opening up almost 1.5 hours per week of time in "non-email" blocks of 30 minutes or more during the regular workday with no Outlook activity (LATE = 2.2 hours).

Treated workers did reduce their time spent in digital applications outside of their regular working hours by about 0.25 hours-per-week (9%), suggesting that some of the time saved on email returned to workers.

We can rule out effect sizes greater than 3.4% of the mean in the ITT estimates for email threads and greater than 4.1% of the mean for reductions in time to reply to emails.

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: Regular Word users with Copilot completed their documents more quickly, consistent with Noy and Zhang (2023), but the ITT point estimate is a savings of only 2% of the pre-period mean, and we cannot rule out that document time to complete increased by as much as 7%.

reported_statistics.verbatim_quote: We estimate that Copilot use also slightly increased the total Word documents authored by the studied workers (we can rule out increases larger than 11% of the pre-period mean and cannot rule out no change).

01_extraction.notes: This is a large-scale field experiment conducted across 66 firms with 7,137 knowledge workers (3,684 treated, 3,453 control based on stated treatment group size). The study measures effects of access to Microsoft 365 Copilot on work patterns using telemetry data from Microsoft Office applications. The main analysis focuses on months 4-6 after treatment assignment (weeks 12+) when usage had stabilized at approximately 40% of treated workers. Over 90% of treated workers used Copilot at least once during the 6-month period. The paper uses difference-in-differences estimation with intent-to-treat (ITT) and local average treatment effect (LATE) specifications, and applies sharpened q-values to account for multiple hypothesis testing. Primary findings: workers with Copilot access spent 1.4-2 fewer hours per week on email (12-17% reduction) and reduced out-of-hours work by 0.25 hours/week (9% reduction), but showed no significant changes in Teams meetings or Word document quantity. The paper notes several limitations: (1) only observes activity within Microsoft Office products; (2) sample may not be representative of broader worker population; (3) Word analysis restricted to workers with above-median pre-period use (>0.25 hours/week); (4) Teams analysis excludes 10 firms primarily using non-Microsoft video conferencing; (5) does not measure email composition time directly; (6) cannot observe content of work or productivity/performance measures; (7) treated workers may have used other generative AI tools (e.g., ChatGPT) during the study period. Spillover effects on coworkers' email activity were modest and generally in the same direction as main effects, with the only statistically significant spillover estimate being on unique email threads replied to (which fell). No negative spillover effects detected on coworkers' work patterns.

02_method.rationale: The study is explicitly described as "A field experiment across 66 firms and 7,137 knowledge workers" with "random assignment to treatment and control groups" where "Treated workers received access to Microsoft 365 Copilot integrated into applications they already used" in their actual work setting for six months. This is randomized assignment conducted in real work environments, not a lab.

per_outcome.operationalization: Total hours per week spent in Outlook sessions, aggregated from telemetry data tracking application usage.
Count of Outlook sessions initiated per week, tracked via telemetry.
Hours per week in continuous non-Outlook blocks ≥30 minutes during working day, derived from telemetry. This measures work pattern/focus time, not speed or quality of output.
Hours per week in Outlook sessions outside individual's defined working hours, tracked via telemetry. This measures work-life balance/overwork, not task speed or quality.
Count of distinct email threads received in a week and replied to within 7 days, from email metadata.
Average hours from email receipt to reply, among emails received that week and replied to within 7 days.
Count of distinct emails read per week, tracked via Outlook telemetry.
Hours per week in scheduled Teams meetings, excluding spontaneous calls, from meeting attendance logs.
Count of Teams meetings attended per week, from meeting attendance logs.
Percentage of meetings joined ≥5 minutes late or left ≥5 minutes early, from meeting join/leave timestamps. This measures meeting engagement/behavior, not output speed or quality.
Hours per week in Word sessions, defined as action blocks with <15 minute gaps, from telemetry.
Average hours from first edit to completion for documents completed that week where worker is primary editor.
Count of documents completed per week where worker is primary editor, from document metadata.
Sum of Outlook, Teams, and Word session hours outside working hours per week. This measures work-life balance/overwork, not output speed or quality.

04_onet.rationale: The study participants are knowledge workers performing email, document creation, video meetings, and chat tasks using Microsoft Office tools. The primary task description explicitly states workers used Copilot 'integrated into applications they already used at work for email, meetings, and writing.' These are quintessential computer-based work activities spanning multiple specific tasks (emailing, word processing, video conferencing). 'Working with Computers' is the most appropriate generalized work activity because it captures the common thread across all measured tasks without overfitting to any single occupation.

alternates.onet_label: Communicating with Supervisors, Peers, or Subordinates

llm_extracted.ai_arm_selection_rationale: Only one AI arm (copilot_access) providing Microsoft 365 Copilot access; control arm represents no AI assistance baseline.
llm_extracted.notes: The paper reports both ITT (intent-to-treat) and LATE (local average treatment effect) estimates. Using ITT estimates: 1.4 fewer hours per week represents a 12% decline. The means provided are: control (pre-period baseline) = 11.7 hours, copilot_access (post-period) = 10.3 hours. The 12% reduction is signed negative because time decreased (smaller is better for time-based outcomes). LATE estimates show 2 fewer hours (17% decline) but ITT is more conservative and standard for primary analysis.
source_quotes: In the pre-period, the average worker spent 11.7 hours per week reading and responding to emails.

Our intent-to-treat (LATE) estimates show workers with access to Copilot spent an average of 1.4 (2) fewer hours per week in email sessions over post-period months 4-6, a 12% (17%) decline from their pre-period average.

computed.notes: The paper reports both ITT (intent-to-treat) and LATE (local average treatment effect) estimates. Using ITT estimates: 1.4 fewer hours per week represents a 12% decline. The means provided are: control (pre-period baseline) = 11.7 hours, copilot_access (post-period) = 10.3 hours. The 12% reduction is signed negative because time decreased (smaller is better for time-based outcomes). LATE estimates show 2 fewer hours (17% decline) but ITT is more conservative and standard for primary analysis. | SDs/SEs missing; variance not computable
